title: LIKE Pattern Matching and NULL Handling

seo.title: SQL LIKE Wildcards and NULL Handling - IS NULL, COALESCE, IFNULL | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Learn SQL pattern matching with LIKE, % and _ wildcards, ESCAPE, case-sensitivity, and NULL handling with IS NULL, IS NOT NULL, COALESCE, IFNULL, NULLIF. 56 runnable MySQL practice questions.
seo.keywords: sql like, sql wildcards, sql pattern matching, sql null handling, sql is null, sql coalesce, sql ifnull, sql nullif, mysql like tutorial, sql case sensitivity

content.what:
What Is Pattern Matching and Why Is NULL Special?

Sometimes you do not want an exact match. You want all students whose name starts with 'A', all emails ending with '@gmail.com', or all products containing the word 'Pro'. That is pattern matching, and SQL provides the LIKE operator with two wildcards (% and _) for exactly this.

The second half of this chapter tackles SQL's most confusing concept: NULL. NULL is not zero, not empty string, not anything — it represents unknown or missing. Comparing anything to NULL does not give TRUE or FALSE — it gives NULL. This breaks beginners' assumptions and causes real production bugs. You must learn IS NULL, IS NOT NULL, and the NULL-handling functions COALESCE, IFNULL, and NULLIF.

Master these two topics and you will write queries that match exactly the rows you want and handle missing data correctly.
content.why:
Why This Matters

1. Search Bars Use LIKE

Every search bar on every website uses LIKE (or full-text search, which builds on LIKE). When you search 'iphone' on Flipkart and it shows iPhone 13, iPhone 15 Pro Max, iPhone Case — that is WHERE name LIKE '%iphone%'.

2. NULL Is Everywhere in Real Data

Your database WILL have NULLs. Optional fields (middle name, secondary email, shipping address), outer joins (missing match on the other side), future-dated fields (end_date for active employees) — all produce NULLs. Not understanding NULL means writing queries that silently skip entire groups of users.

3. Failing the NULL Interview Question

"What is the difference between WHERE salary = NULL and WHERE salary IS NULL?" is asked in every intermediate SQL interview. If you answer wrong, the interview is over. Production bugs have cost companies millions because of this single misunderstanding.

4. COALESCE Is Your Best Friend

When a user's middle_name is NULL, you want to display the first name only, not "Aarav NULL Sharma". COALESCE(middle_name, '') returns empty string if NULL. This function appears in 90% of real SELECT queries that build display strings.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

Reference Table: students

We will use a students table for this chapter. Notice the intentional NULLs.

CREATE TABLE students (
    id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(50),
    email VARCHAR(100),
    course VARCHAR(40),
    city VARCHAR(30),
    marks INT
);

INSERT INTO students VALUES
(1, 'Aarav Sharma', 'aarav@gmail.com', 'Computer Science', 'Mumbai', 85),
(2, 'Priya Patel', 'priya.p@yahoo.com', 'Mechanical', 'Ahmedabad', 72),
(3, 'Rohan Gupta', 'rohan@gmail.com', 'Computer Science', 'Delhi', 91),
(4, 'Ananya Singh', NULL, 'Electronics', 'Bengaluru', 78),
(5, 'Vikram Reddy', 'vikram.r@outlook.com', 'Computer Science', 'Hyderabad', NULL),
(6, 'Sneha Iyer', 'sneha@gmail.com', 'Civil', NULL, 65),
(7, 'Karan Mehta', 'karan_m@yahoo.com', 'Mechanical', 'Mumbai', 88),
(8, 'Ishita Kumar', NULL, 'Electronics', 'Delhi', NULL),
(9, 'Aditya Rao', 'aditya@gmail.com', 'Computer Science', 'Pune', 70),
(10, 'Diya Nair', 'diya_n@outlook.com', 'Civil', 'Chennai', 82);

1. The LIKE Operator Basics

LIKE performs pattern matching on strings. Use it in WHERE clauses with two wildcards:

  • % matches zero or more of any character
  • _ matches exactly one of any character

Common Patterns

-- Names starting with 'A'
WHERE name LIKE 'A%'

-- Names ending with 'a'
WHERE name LIKE '%a'

-- Names containing 'ro'
WHERE name LIKE '%ro%'

-- Names starting with 'A' and having exactly 5 chars total
WHERE name LIKE 'A____'  -- 'A' plus 4 underscores = 5 chars

-- Second letter is 'o'
WHERE name LIKE '_o%'

2. Concrete Examples on students

-- Names starting with 'A'
SELECT name FROM students WHERE name LIKE 'A%';
-- Returns: Aarav Sharma, Ananya Singh, Aditya Rao (3 rows)

-- Gmail addresses
SELECT name, email FROM students
WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com';
-- Returns: Aarav, Rohan, Sneha, Aditya (4 rows)

-- Names with exactly 11 characters
SELECT name FROM students WHERE name LIKE '___________';
-- 11 underscores — matches Priya Patel, Sneha Iyer, Diya Nair

3. NOT LIKE

Invert LIKE with NOT:

-- Non-gmail addresses
SELECT name, email FROM students
WHERE email NOT LIKE '%@gmail.com';

4. ESCAPE for Literal % or _

What if you want to search for a literal % (like in discount codes)? Use ESCAPE:

-- Match strings containing '50%' literally
WHERE coupon LIKE '%50\%%' ESCAPE '\\'

-- Or choose your own escape character
WHERE coupon LIKE '%50!%%' ESCAPE '!'

The ESCAPE clause tells SQL: "treat this character as escape." The character immediately after it is literal, not a wildcard.

5. Case Sensitivity

This is database-dependent and important:

  • MySQL: LIKE is case-insensitive by default for VARCHAR columns (using the default collation utf8mb4_0900_ai_ci — 'ci' = case-insensitive). WHERE name LIKE 'a%' matches 'Aarav'.
  • PostgreSQL: LIKE is case-sensitive. WHERE name LIKE 'a%' will NOT match 'Aarav'. Use ILIKE for case-insensitive matching (PostgreSQL only).
  • SQL Server: Depends on collation. Default is case-insensitive.

For portable, case-insensitive matching, wrap both sides in LOWER():

WHERE LOWER(name) LIKE LOWER('a%')

6. Performance Note

Patterns starting with % (like '%gmail.com') cannot use a regular B-tree index — the database must scan every row. Patterns anchored at the start ('Aarav%') CAN use an index. For high-volume text search with leading wildcards, use a full-text index (covered in advanced chapters).

7. NULL — The Three-Valued Logic

SQL uses three-valued logic: TRUE, FALSE, and NULL (unknown). Most programming languages have only TRUE/FALSE. In SQL:

  • 5 = 5 is TRUE
  • 5 = 3 is FALSE
  • 5 = NULL is NULL (not FALSE!)
  • NULL = NULL is NULL (not TRUE!)

The WHERE clause keeps only rows where the condition is TRUE. NULL is NOT TRUE, so rows with NULL in comparisons are silently dropped.

8. IS NULL and IS NOT NULL

Since = NULL never works, SQL provides dedicated operators:

-- Find students with missing email
SELECT name FROM students WHERE email IS NULL;
-- Returns: Ananya Singh, Ishita Kumar

-- Find students with emails
SELECT name FROM students WHERE email IS NOT NULL;
-- Returns: the other 8 students

NEVER write WHERE email = NULL — it always returns 0 rows.

9. COALESCE — First Non-NULL

COALESCE(val1, val2, val3, ...) returns the first non-NULL value from the argument list.

-- Show email, or 'No email' if NULL
SELECT name, COALESCE(email, 'No email') AS contact FROM students;

-- Show marks, or 0 if NULL
SELECT name, COALESCE(marks, 0) AS marks FROM students;

COALESCE is standard SQL — works on every database. Use it over IFNULL for portability.

10. IFNULL — MySQL-Specific Shortcut

IFNULL(value, default) is MySQL's 2-argument version:

SELECT IFNULL(marks, 0) FROM students;
-- Equivalent to COALESCE(marks, 0)

IFNULL only takes 2 arguments. COALESCE takes any number. For portability, prefer COALESCE.

11. NULLIF — The Opposite

NULLIF(a, b) returns NULL if a = b, otherwise returns a.

-- Turn empty string into NULL (common data cleaning trick)
SELECT NULLIF(email, '') FROM students;

-- Avoid division by zero
SELECT marks / NULLIF(total_marks, 0) FROM students;
-- If total_marks is 0, NULLIF returns NULL, and dividing by NULL gives NULL (safer than error)

12. NULL in Aggregate Functions

Aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX) skip NULLs — except COUNT(*):

  • COUNT(*) — all rows
  • COUNT(column) — non-NULL rows
  • SUM(column) — sum of non-NULL values
  • AVG(column) — average of non-NULL values (NULL not treated as 0)

This matters: if you want NULLs counted as 0 in an AVG, you must handle them explicitly: AVG(COALESCE(marks, 0)).

13. NULL in Arithmetic and Concatenation

Any arithmetic with NULL yields NULL. 5 + NULL = NULL. NULL * 10 = NULL. String concatenation with NULL also gives NULL: CONCAT('Hello ', NULL) = NULL.

This is why you wrap NULL-able columns with COALESCE in display strings:

SELECT CONCAT(first_name, ' ', COALESCE(middle_name, ''), ' ', last_name) AS full_name FROM users;

codeExamples.title: Basic LIKE Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: % matches zero or more characters. 'A%' means 'starts with A'. '%a' means 'ends with a'. '%an%' means 'contains an'. Note MySQL is case-insensitive by default, so 'A%' would also match names starting with lowercase 'a'.
codeExamples.output:
Starting with 'A' (3 rows):
Aarav Sharma
Ananya Singh
Aditya Rao

Ending with 'a' (4 rows):
Aarav Sharma (no - ends in 'a'? Actually 'Sharma' ends in 'a')
Priya Patel (no - ends in 'l')
Wait: 'Sharma' ends in 'a', 'Patel' ends in 'l', 'Gupta' ends in 'a', 'Singh' ends in 'h'...
Correct: Aarav Sharma, Rohan Gupta, Ishita Kumar (no 'r'), Diya Nair (r)... 
Actually: ending in 'a': Sharma, Gupta, only 2.
Simpler - names actually ending with 'a': Aarav Sharma, Rohan Gupta. 2 rows.

Containing 'an' (2 rows):
Ananya Singh, Ishita Kumar (no 'an'), Karan Mehta (yes - 'an')
Ananya (an twice), Karan (an) - 2 rows.

codeExamples.title: Underscore Wildcard
codeExamples.explanation: _ matches exactly one character. '_o%': first char anything, second char 'o', then zero or more. '___________': exactly 11 underscores means exactly 11 characters. 'Priya Patel' has 11 chars (including space).
codeExamples.output:
Second letter 'o' (3 rows):
Rohan Gupta
Sneha Iyer (no - 'n' is second)
Aditya Rao - no, 'd' is second
Actually: only 'Rohan' has o as second letter. Wait, looking closer... 
Rohan Gupta: R-o-h-a-n, yes 'o' second. Match.
So: Rohan Gupta (1 row).

Exactly 11 chars:
Priya Patel (11 with space), Sneha Iyer (10 - no), Diya Nair (9 - no)
Actually 'Priya Patel' = 11. 'Karan Mehta' = 11.
(2 rows)

codeExamples.title: Matching Email Domains
codeExamples.code:
-- Gmail users
SELECT name, email FROM students
WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com';

-- Non-gmail users
SELECT name, email FROM students
WHERE email NOT LIKE '%@gmail.com'
  AND email IS NOT NULL;
codeExamples.explanation: The gmail pattern: '%@gmail.com' means anything followed by @gmail.com. For non-gmail, we use NOT LIKE. Notice we also filter out NULL emails explicitly — NOT LIKE on a NULL value returns NULL, not TRUE, so NULL emails are already excluded, but adding IS NOT NULL makes the intent clearer.
codeExamples.output:
Gmail users (4 rows):
Aarav Sharma, aarav@gmail.com
Rohan Gupta, rohan@gmail.com
Sneha Iyer, sneha@gmail.com
Aditya Rao, aditya@gmail.com

Non-gmail (4 rows):
Priya Patel, priya.p@yahoo.com
Vikram Reddy, vikram.r@outlook.com
Karan Mehta, karan_m@yahoo.com
Diya Nair, diya_n@outlook.com

codeExamples.title: NULL Comparison Pitfall
codeExamples.explanation: Comparing anything with NULL using = or != returns NULL (three-valued logic). WHERE only keeps rows where the condition is TRUE, so NULL results are dropped. The first two queries return 0 rows. The CORRECT queries use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL. This is the #1 NULL gotcha in SQL.
codeExamples.output:
Query 1 (email = NULL): 0 rows (bug!)
Query 2 (email != NULL): 0 rows (bug!)
Query 3 (IS NULL): Ananya Singh, Ishita Kumar (2 rows)
Query 4 (IS NOT NULL): the other 8 students

codeExamples.title: COALESCE to Handle Missing Data
codeExamples.code:
-- Replace NULL email with 'No email on file'
SELECT 
    name,
    COALESCE(email, 'No email on file') AS contact,
    COALESCE(marks, 0) AS marks,
    COALESCE(city, 'Unknown') AS city
FROM students;
codeExamples.explanation: COALESCE returns the first non-NULL value. For each student, if email is NULL, show 'No email on file' instead. If marks is NULL, show 0. If city is NULL, show 'Unknown'. This is how you produce clean reports even with missing data.
codeExamples.output:
name           contact              marks  city
Aarav Sharma   aarav@gmail.com      85     Mumbai
Priya Patel    priya.p@yahoo.com    72     Ahmedabad
Rohan Gupta    rohan@gmail.com      91     Delhi
Ananya Singh   No email on file    78     Bengaluru
Vikram Reddy   vikram.r@outlook.com 0      Hyderabad
Sneha Iyer     sneha@gmail.com      65     Unknown
Karan Mehta    karan_m@yahoo.com    88     Mumbai
Ishita Kumar   No email on file    0      Delhi
Aditya Rao     aditya@gmail.com     70     Pune
Diya Nair      diya_n@outlook.com   82     Chennai

codeExamples.title: IFNULL (MySQL shortcut)
codeExamples.code:
-- IFNULL is the MySQL 2-arg version of COALESCE
SELECT
    name,
    IFNULL(email, 'N/A') AS email,
    IFNULL(marks, 0) AS marks
FROM students
WHERE id IN (4, 5, 8);
codeExamples.explanation: IFNULL(value, default) returns default if value is NULL. It is equivalent to COALESCE(value, default) but only accepts 2 arguments and is MySQL-specific. Use COALESCE for portable code. We filter to ids 4, 5, 8 — the three students with NULLs.
codeExamples.output:
name           email   marks
Ananya Singh   N/A    78
Vikram Reddy   vikram.r@outlook.com  0
Ishita Kumar   N/A    0

codeExamples.title: NULLIF for Safe Division
codeExamples.code:
-- Assume total_marks column exists; avoid divide-by-zero
-- NULLIF(x, 0) returns NULL when x = 0

-- Illustrative example (we do not have total_marks in students)
SELECT 
    marks,
    NULLIF(marks, 0) AS safe_marks,
    marks / NULLIF(marks, 100) AS ratio_if_not_100
FROM students
WHERE id IN (1, 3);
codeExamples.explanation: NULLIF(a, b) returns NULL if a = b, otherwise a. Useful to prevent divide-by-zero by replacing 0 with NULL (and NULL division gives NULL, not an error). The second expression: if marks = 100, returns NULL; else, marks / marks = 1. For our data, marks are 85 and 91, not 100, so the ratio is 1.

codeExamples.title: Pattern Matching with Underscore: Exact Position
codeExamples.explanation: '__a%': any 2 chars, then 'a', then anything. Matches: Aaarav? Let us check A-a-r-a-v: position 3 is 'r'. So NO. What about Ishita: I-s-h... no 'a' at position 3. Let us test Aarav: 1=A, 2=a, 3=r — no. Ishita: I-s-h-i-t-a, 3='h', no. Priya: P-r-i-y-a, 3='i', no. Karan: K-a-r-a-n, 3='r', no. Hmm, tricky. Maybe Diya: D-i-y-a, 3='y', no. So possibly 0 rows! '_r%': position 2 is 'r'. Priya (Pr...), no wait P-r yes 2='r'. Sharma starts with S. 'Priya Patel' position 2='r'. Matches just Priya.
codeExamples.output:
Pattern '__a%' (3rd char is 'a'): examining carefully:
'Ananya Singh': A-n-a-n-y-a - pos 3 = 'a'. MATCH.
'Sneha Iyer': S-n-e-h-a... pos 3 = 'e'. No.
'Diya Nair': D-i-y-a, pos 3 = 'y'. No.
Result: Ananya Singh (1 row).

Pattern '_r%' (2nd char 'r'):
Priya Patel: P-r... match!
(1 row)

codeExamples.title: NULL in Aggregates
codeExamples.code:
-- marks has 2 NULLs (Vikram id 5, Ishita id 8)
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
    COUNT(marks) AS marks_count,
    AVG(marks) AS avg_marks_ignoring_nulls,
    AVG(COALESCE(marks, 0)) AS avg_marks_null_as_zero
FROM students;
codeExamples.explanation: COUNT(*) = 10 (all rows). COUNT(marks) = 8 (excludes 2 NULLs). AVG(marks) averages 8 non-NULL values: (85+72+91+78+65+88+70+82)/8 = 631/8 = 78.875. AVG(COALESCE(marks, 0)) treats NULLs as 0: 631/10 = 63.1. Huge difference — know which one you want!

commonMistakes.title: Using = NULL Instead of IS NULL
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
-- Finding students without email
SELECT name FROM students WHERE email = NULL;
commonMistakes.error: No error, but returns ZERO rows even when students have NULL email. Silent bug.
commonMistakes.correctCode: SELECT name FROM students WHERE email IS NULL;
commonMistakes.explanation: = NULL evaluates to NULL, not TRUE. WHERE requires TRUE. So the query returns 0 rows. Always use IS NULL and IS NOT NULL to test for NULL. This is the single most common SQL bug.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting Case Sensitivity in PostgreSQL
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
-- In PostgreSQL, this misses 'Aarav'
SELECT name FROM students WHERE name LIKE 'aarav%';
commonMistakes.error: In PostgreSQL and some MySQL configurations, LIKE is case-sensitive. This returns 0 rows. In MySQL default collation, it works.
commonMistakes.explanation: MySQL is case-insensitive by default (depends on column collation). PostgreSQL is case-sensitive and requires ILIKE for case-insensitive matching. Always be explicit when writing portable code: use LOWER() on both sides.

commonMistakes.title: Using LIKE Without Wildcards
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
-- Rohan expects this to find 'Aarav Sharma'
SELECT name FROM students WHERE name LIKE 'Aarav';
commonMistakes.error: No error, but returns 0 rows unless there is a student named exactly 'Aarav' (no last name).
commonMistakes.explanation: LIKE without wildcards behaves like =, matching the exact string. Most students expect LIKE to mean 'contains' by default, but it does not. You must add % wildcards. Without wildcards, LIKE 'Aarav' matches only the string 'Aarav' exactly.

commonMistakes.title: NULL Concatenation Producing NULL
commonMistakes.error: No error, but for users with NULL middle_name, full_name is NULL (the entire concatenated string). Not 'Aarav  Sharma' — just NULL.
commonMistakes.explanation: In MySQL's CONCAT, any NULL argument makes the result NULL. Use COALESCE to substitute empty strings, or use CONCAT_WS (concat with separator) which skips NULLs. This bug appears in production whenever any optional text field is displayed.

commonMistakes.title: NOT IN with NULL in the Subquery
commonMistakes.error: No error, but if any row in banned has NULL student_id, this returns 0 students. Production bug.
commonMistakes.explanation: The same NOT IN + NULL problem from chapter 6 reappears with subqueries. A NULL in the subquery result turns id != NULL into NULL, and WHERE drops those rows. NOT EXISTS is the bulletproof alternative.

summary: LIKE does pattern matching with two wildcards: % matches any sequence (including empty) of characters, _ matches exactly one character.
Common patterns: 'A%' starts with A, '%a' ends with a, '%an%' contains an, 'A_____' is exactly 6 chars starting with A.
NOT LIKE inverts LIKE. Combine with IS NOT NULL when dealing with nullable columns to avoid surprises.
MySQL LIKE is case-insensitive by default (default collation). PostgreSQL LIKE is case-sensitive; use ILIKE or LOWER() for case-insensitive.
Use ESCAPE to match literal % or _ characters: WHERE col LIKE '%50\%%' ESCAPE '\\'.
NULL is not zero, not empty string — it is 'unknown.' Any comparison with NULL yields NULL, not TRUE or FALSE.
WHERE salary = NULL returns 0 rows even if NULLs exist. Always use IS NULL and IS NOT NULL instead.
COALESCE(v1, v2, ...) returns the first non-NULL argument. Use it to substitute defaults for missing data.
IFNULL(value, default) is MySQL's 2-argument version of COALESCE. NULLIF(a, b) returns NULL if a = b (useful for avoiding divide-by-zero).
Aggregates (SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX) skip NULLs. COUNT(*) includes all rows; COUNT(col) excludes NULLs. Arithmetic with NULL yields NULL.

topicSpecific.question: Find all students whose name starts with 'A'.
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT name FROM students WHERE name LIKE 'A%';
topicSpecific.explanation: Matches Aarav, Ananya, Aditya — 3 rows.

topicSpecific.question: Find all students whose name ends with 'a'.
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT name FROM students WHERE name LIKE '%a';
topicSpecific.explanation: In our data: Aarav Sharma, Rohan Gupta, Ishita Kumar (no — ends in 'r'), Karan Mehta. Ending in 'a': Sharma, Gupta, Mehta. 3 rows: Aarav Sharma, Rohan Gupta, Karan Mehta.

topicSpecific.question: Find all students whose email is NULL.
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT name FROM students WHERE email IS NULL;
topicSpecific.explanation: Ananya Singh and Ishita Kumar have NULL emails. 2 rows. Using = NULL would return 0 rows.

topicSpecific.question: Find students with a gmail email address.
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT name, email FROM students
WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com';
topicSpecific.explanation: Aarav, Rohan, Sneha, Aditya — 4 rows.

topicSpecific.question:
How many rows?
SELECT name FROM students WHERE name LIKE '%ya%';
topicSpecific.answer: 3 rows
topicSpecific.explanation: Contains 'ya': Priya, Aditya, Diya. 3 rows. Other names do not have 'ya'.

topicSpecific.question: Find students with missing marks.
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT name FROM students WHERE marks IS NULL;
topicSpecific.explanation: Vikram Reddy and Ishita Kumar have NULL marks. 2 rows.

topicSpecific.question: Find students from a city that starts with 'M'.
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT name, city FROM students WHERE city LIKE 'M%';
topicSpecific.explanation: Mumbai starts with M. Aarav (Mumbai) and Karan (Mumbai). 2 rows.

topicSpecific.question: Find students whose email is NOT NULL.
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT name FROM students WHERE email IS NOT NULL;
topicSpecific.explanation: 8 students have emails. Only Ananya and Ishita are excluded.

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between % and _ in LIKE patterns?
topicSpecific.answer: % matches zero or more characters (any length). _ matches exactly one character. So 'A%' matches 'A', 'Aa', 'Aarav', 'Apple' — any length starting with A. 'A_' matches only 2-character strings like 'Ab', 'Az', 'A1'. 'A__' matches exactly 3 characters.
topicSpecific.explanation: These are the only two wildcards in standard LIKE. Regex is a separate feature (REGEXP in MySQL, ~ in PostgreSQL).

topicSpecific.question: Why does WHERE email = NULL return zero rows?
topicSpecific.answer: Because NULL means 'unknown,' any comparison with NULL evaluates to NULL (not TRUE, not FALSE). The WHERE clause only keeps rows where the condition is TRUE. So email = NULL is NULL for every row, and no row passes the filter. The correct syntax is WHERE email IS NULL.
topicSpecific.explanation: This is SQL's #1 NULL gotcha and a frequent interview question. It trips up every beginner.

topicSpecific.question: Find students with names having exactly 10 characters.
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT name FROM students
WHERE name LIKE '__________';  -- 10 underscores
topicSpecific.explanation: Each underscore matches one character. Names with exactly 10 chars: 'Sneha Iyer' (10), 'Aarav Sharma' (12 - no). 'Diya Nair' = 9, 'Aditya Rao' = 10, 'Rohan Gupta' = 11, 'Sneha Iyer' = 10. Approx 2 rows (Aditya Rao, Sneha Iyer).

topicSpecific.question: Show name and email, replacing NULL emails with 'Not provided'. Use COALESCE.
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT name, COALESCE(email, 'Not provided') AS contact
FROM students;
topicSpecific.explanation: All 10 rows returned. For Ananya and Ishita, the contact column shows 'Not provided'. For others, the real email.

topicSpecific.question: Find students who do NOT have a yahoo email. Remember to handle NULLs.
topicSpecific.hint: NOT LIKE plus IS NOT NULL.
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT name, email FROM students
WHERE email NOT LIKE '%@yahoo.com'
  AND email IS NOT NULL;
topicSpecific.explanation: Non-yahoo emails: gmail (4), outlook (2). Total 6 rows. NULL emails (Ananya, Ishita) are excluded explicitly — otherwise their NOT LIKE evaluates to NULL and they are dropped anyway, but being explicit is clearer.

topicSpecific.question: Find students whose name second letter is 'a' or 'i' (use LIKE with underscore).
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT name FROM students
WHERE name LIKE '_a%' OR name LIKE '_i%';
topicSpecific.explanation: Second char 'a': Karan, Sharma (first name Aarav), Vikram... Let me check: 'Aarav' 2nd = 'a'. 'Karan' 2nd = 'a'. 'Vikram' 2nd = 'i'. 'Diya' 2nd = 'i'. 'Vikram' matches 2nd letter 'i'. Aarav, Karan, Vikram, Diya — approx 4 rows.

topicSpecific.question: Show name and marks. If marks is NULL, show 0. Use IFNULL.
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT name, IFNULL(marks, 0) AS marks FROM students;
topicSpecific.explanation: All 10 rows. Vikram and Ishita show 0 instead of NULL. Others show their actual marks.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this return?
SELECT COALESCE(NULL, NULL, 'Hello', 'World');
topicSpecific.answer: Hello
topicSpecific.explanation: COALESCE returns the first non-NULL argument. NULL, NULL are skipped. 'Hello' is first non-NULL.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this return?
SELECT NULLIF(10, 10), NULLIF(10, 5);
topicSpecific.hint: NULLIF returns NULL when args equal.
topicSpecific.answer: NULL, 10
topicSpecific.explanation: NULLIF(10, 10): args equal, returns NULL. NULLIF(10, 5): args differ, returns the first arg (10).

topicSpecific.question: Find students whose email contains an underscore. Remember _ is a wildcard — you need to escape it.
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT name, email FROM students
WHERE email LIKE '%\_%' ESCAPE '\\';

-- Or using a different escape char:
SELECT name, email FROM students
WHERE email LIKE '%!_%' ESCAPE '!';
topicSpecific.explanation: Emails with literal underscore in local-part: karan_m@yahoo.com, diya_n@outlook.com. 2 rows. Without ESCAPE, the _ would match ANY character and the query would match all emails.

topicSpecific.question: Find students from Mumbai OR whose city is missing.
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT name, city FROM students
WHERE city = 'Mumbai' OR city IS NULL;
topicSpecific.explanation: Mumbai: Aarav, Karan. NULL city: Sneha. Total 3 rows.

topicSpecific.question: Explain when LIKE can use an index and when it cannot.
topicSpecific.answer: LIKE can use a B-tree index when the pattern is anchored at the start: LIKE 'Aarav%'. The index can quickly find all rows starting with 'Aarav'. LIKE CANNOT use a B-tree index when the pattern starts with a wildcard: LIKE '%gmail'. The database must scan every row. For leading-wildcard searches at scale, use a full-text index or trigram index.
topicSpecific.explanation: This is why search bars on large sites use full-text indexes like MySQL's FULLTEXT, Elasticsearch, or PostgreSQL's trigram (pg_trgm) extension. Simple LIKE with leading wildcard is O(n).

topicSpecific.question:
What does this return?
SELECT 5 + NULL, NULL * 10, CONCAT('Hi ', NULL);
topicSpecific.answer: NULL, NULL, NULL
topicSpecific.explanation: Any arithmetic or string operation with NULL yields NULL. This is three-valued logic in action. To prevent this, wrap NULL columns in COALESCE.

topicSpecific.question: Find students whose name has exactly 5 characters in the first word (before the space). E.g., 'Aarav' has 5 chars.
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT name FROM students
WHERE name LIKE '_____ %';
topicSpecific.explanation: 5 underscores, then a space, then any last name. Matches: 'Aarav Sharma' (Aarav = 5), 'Priya Patel' (5), 'Rohan Gupta' (5), 'Karan Mehta' (5), 'Diya Nair' (4 - no), 'Sneha Iyer' (5). So 5 rows (Aarav, Priya, Rohan, Karan, Sneha).

topicSpecific.question: Show name, marks, and a status column: 'Pass' if marks >= 60, 'Fail' if marks < 60, 'Not Attempted' if marks is NULL. Use CASE with IS NULL.
topicSpecific.hint: CASE WHEN marks IS NULL... WHEN marks >= 60... ELSE...
topicSpecific.answer:
SELECT name, marks,
  CASE
    WHEN marks IS NULL THEN 'Not Attempted'
    WHEN marks >= 60 THEN 'Pass'
    ELSE 'Fail'
  END AS status
FROM students;
topicSpecific.explanation: CASE evaluates top-to-bottom, returns the first match. NULL branch must come first — otherwise marks >= 60 on NULL gives NULL (not TRUE), and the row falls through to ELSE (Fail), which is wrong. All 10 rows, with Vikram and Ishita showing 'Not Attempted'.

topicSpecific.question: Find average marks treating NULLs as 0 (not as missing).
topicSpecific.answer: SELECT AVG(COALESCE(marks, 0)) AS avg_marks FROM students;
topicSpecific.explanation: Sum of non-NULL marks = 85+72+91+78+65+88+70+82 = 631. Total rows = 10. AVG with NULLs as 0: 631/10 = 63.1. Contrast with AVG(marks) which ignores NULLs and divides by 8: 631/8 = 78.875.

topicSpecific.question: Why might an application team prefer NULLs over sentinel values like 0 or empty string?
topicSpecific.answer: NULL explicitly means 'unknown' or 'not applicable.' A 0 in marks could mean the student scored zero OR did not take the test — ambiguous. An empty string in email could mean 'no email' OR 'user has not been prompted yet.' NULL keeps these cases distinguishable. Aggregate functions also handle NULLs sensibly by ignoring them (AVG skips missing data instead of skewing the average with zeros).
topicSpecific.explanation: The tradeoff: NULLs require care in WHERE clauses and aggregates. Some teams use sentinel values to avoid NULL complexity, but at the cost of semantic clarity.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this return?
SELECT NULL = NULL, NULL != NULL;
topicSpecific.answer: NULL, NULL
topicSpecific.explanation: Any comparison with NULL — even NULL to NULL — yields NULL, not TRUE or FALSE. This is why IS NULL and IS NOT NULL exist. It is the definitional behavior of SQL's three-valued logic.

mixed.question: Find all students in the Computer Science course.
mixed.answer: SELECT * FROM students WHERE course = 'Computer Science';
mixed.explanation: Aarav, Rohan, Vikram, Aditya — 4 rows.

mixed.question: Find students whose name starts with 'P' or 'S'.
mixed.answer:
SELECT name FROM students
WHERE name LIKE 'P%' OR name LIKE 'S%';
mixed.explanation: Priya Patel, Sneha Iyer. 2 rows.

mixed.question: Find students whose name contains 'sh' anywhere (case-insensitive in MySQL).
mixed.answer: SELECT name FROM students WHERE name LIKE '%sh%';
mixed.explanation: Names with 'sh': 'Aarav Sharma' (has 'Sh'), 'Ishita Kumar' (has 'sh'). 2 rows. MySQL is case-insensitive, so 'Sh' matches 'sh%'.

mixed.question: Find students whose city is NULL.
mixed.answer: SELECT name FROM students WHERE city IS NULL;
mixed.explanation: Only Sneha Iyer has NULL city. 1 row.

mixed.question: What does IFNULL(NULL, 'N/A') return?
mixed.answer: 'N/A'
mixed.explanation: IFNULL returns the 2nd argument when the 1st is NULL. Here, NULL triggers the default 'N/A'.

mixed.question: Find students with non-NULL marks.
mixed.answer: SELECT * FROM students WHERE marks IS NOT NULL;
mixed.explanation: 8 students with marks. Vikram and Ishita excluded.

mixed.question:
How many rows?
SELECT * FROM students WHERE name LIKE '%';
mixed.answer: 10 rows
mixed.explanation: % alone matches any non-NULL string, including empty strings. All 10 students have names, so all are returned.

mixed.question: List students with exactly 11-character names. Use _.
mixed.answer: SELECT name FROM students WHERE name LIKE '___________';
mixed.explanation: 11 chars including spaces: 'Priya Patel' (11), 'Karan Mehta' (11), 'Rohan Gupta' (11). 3 rows.

mixed.question: Show name and city, showing 'Unknown' for missing cities.
mixed.answer: SELECT name, COALESCE(city, 'Unknown') AS city FROM students;
mixed.explanation: All 10 rows. Sneha shows 'Unknown' for city; others show actual city.

mixed.question: Find students whose course starts with 'C'.
mixed.answer: SELECT name, course FROM students WHERE course LIKE 'C%';
mixed.explanation: 'Computer Science' starts with C, 'Civil' starts with C. Matches: Aarav, Rohan, Vikram, Sneha (Civil), Aditya, Diya (Civil). 6 rows.

mixed.question:
What does this return?
SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(email), COUNT(marks) FROM students;
mixed.answer: 10, 8, 8
mixed.explanation: COUNT(*) = 10 (all rows). COUNT(email) = 8 (2 NULLs). COUNT(marks) = 8 (2 NULLs).

mixed.question: Find students whose name contains 'Kumar' OR 'Nair' using LIKE.
mixed.answer:
SELECT name FROM students
WHERE name LIKE '%Kumar%' OR name LIKE '%Nair%';
mixed.explanation: Ishita Kumar, Diya Nair. 2 rows.

mixed.question: Find students in Mumbai or Delhi with gmail emails.
mixed.answer:
SELECT name, email, city FROM students
WHERE city IN ('Mumbai', 'Delhi')
  AND email LIKE '%@gmail.com';
mixed.explanation: Mumbai gmail: Aarav. Delhi gmail: Rohan. 2 rows. Karan is Mumbai with yahoo, so excluded.

mixed.question:
What does this return?
SELECT AVG(marks), AVG(COALESCE(marks, 0)) FROM students;
mixed.explanation: AVG(marks) ignores NULLs: sum 631 / 8 rows = 78.875. AVG(COALESCE(marks, 0)) counts NULL as 0: sum 631 / 10 rows = 63.1.

mixed.question: Show each student's name and a 'has_email' flag ('Yes' or 'No') using IFNULL.
mixed.hint: CASE or combining IFNULL with comparison.
mixed.answer:
SELECT name,
  IF(email IS NULL, 'No', 'Yes') AS has_email
FROM students;

-- Or using CASE:
SELECT name,
  CASE WHEN email IS NULL THEN 'No' ELSE 'Yes' END AS has_email
FROM students;
mixed.explanation: MySQL's IF(cond, a, b) returns a if cond is TRUE, else b. Ananya and Ishita show 'No'; others 'Yes'. All 10 rows.

mixed.question: Find students with exactly 4-character first names. Split by space, check the first word.
mixed.answer: SELECT name FROM students WHERE name LIKE '____ %';
mixed.explanation: 4 underscores, space, anything. First names of 4 chars: 'Diya' (D-i-y-a), 'Aarav' (5 - no), 'Priya' (5 - no). Just Diya Nair. 1 row.

mixed.question: Find students whose email has an underscore in the local-part (before @). Use ESCAPE.
mixed.answer:
SELECT name, email FROM students
WHERE email LIKE '%\_%@%' ESCAPE '\\';
mixed.explanation: karan_m@yahoo.com and diya_n@outlook.com. 2 rows. The ESCAPE clause ensures \_ is literal, not a wildcard.

mixed.question: Find students whose email domain is NOT gmail.com and whose marks are either NULL or above 80.
mixed.hint: Three conditions: not gmail, AND (NULL OR high marks).
mixed.answer:
SELECT name, email, marks FROM students
WHERE email NOT LIKE '%@gmail.com'
  AND email IS NOT NULL
  AND (marks IS NULL OR marks > 80);
mixed.explanation: Non-gmail with NULL or marks > 80: Vikram (NULL marks, outlook), Karan (88, yahoo), Diya (82, outlook). 3 rows. Priya (yahoo, 72) fails marks check.

mixed.question: Display a full contact info column: name (email, marks). Use COALESCE for NULLs.
mixed.answer:
SELECT CONCAT(
    name, ' (',
    COALESCE(email, 'No email'), ', ',
    COALESCE(CAST(marks AS CHAR), 'No marks'),
    ')'
) AS contact_info
FROM students;
mixed.explanation: Without COALESCE, any NULL would make the whole CONCAT NULL. We CAST marks to CHAR because CONCAT needs a string and COALESCE needs same types. Ananya shows 'No email', Vikram shows 'No marks', Ishita shows both.

mixed.question:
What does this return?
SELECT NULLIF(CONCAT(name, ' ', email), CONCAT(name, ' '))
FROM students WHERE id = 4;
mixed.answer: NULL
mixed.explanation: Ananya's email is NULL, so CONCAT(name, ' ', NULL) = NULL. NULLIF(NULL, anything) = NULL. The result is NULL.

mixed.question: Find students whose name has 'a' as exactly the 2nd character AND whose city is known (not NULL).
mixed.answer:
SELECT name, city FROM students
WHERE name LIKE '_a%'
  AND city IS NOT NULL;
mixed.explanation: 2nd char 'a': Aarav (A-a-...), Karan (K-a-...). Both have cities. 2 rows (Aarav Mumbai, Karan Mumbai).

mixed.question: Show each student and their marks grade: 'A' if >= 85, 'B' if >= 70, 'C' if < 70, 'Absent' if NULL. Use CASE.
mixed.hint: CASE with multiple WHEN, NULL check first.
mixed.answer:
SELECT name, marks,
  CASE
    WHEN marks IS NULL THEN 'Absent'
    WHEN marks >= 85 THEN 'A'
    WHEN marks >= 70 THEN 'B'
    ELSE 'C'
  END AS grade
FROM students;
mixed.explanation: Always check NULL first to avoid falling through. Aarav 85=A, Priya 72=B, Rohan 91=A, Ananya 78=B, Vikram NULL=Absent, Sneha 65=C, Karan 88=A, Ishita NULL=Absent, Aditya 70=B, Diya 82=B. 10 rows.

mcqs.question: What does % wildcard match in LIKE?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. % matches zero or more of any characters. 'A%' matches 'A', 'Aa', 'Aardvark', etc.

mcqs.question: What does _ wildcard match?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Underscore matches exactly one character. 'A_' matches any 2-char string starting with A.

mcqs.question: How do you check if a column is NULL?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Only IS NULL works. Comparing with = NULL returns NULL (not TRUE), so the row is dropped by WHERE.

mcqs.question: What does COALESCE(NULL, NULL, 5, NULL, 10) return?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. COALESCE returns the FIRST non-NULL argument. NULLs are skipped, first non-NULL is 5.

mcqs.question: What does WHERE name LIKE 'A%' match?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. 'A%' means 'A' followed by anything — starts with A.

mcqs.question: What does 5 + NULL return?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Any arithmetic with NULL yields NULL. NULL propagates through expressions.

mcqs.question: In MySQL (default collation), does LIKE 'a%' match 'Aarav'?

options.text: Yes, MySQL LIKE is case-insensitive by default

mcqs.explanation: A is correct. MySQL's default collation utf8mb4_0900_ai_ci is case-insensitive. PostgreSQL's LIKE is case-sensitive and requires ILIKE for case-insensitive.

mcqs.question: What does NULLIF(5, 5) return?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. NULLIF(a, b) returns NULL if a = b, else returns a. Here 5 = 5, so NULL.

mcqs.question: How many rows does SELECT * FROM students WHERE email = NULL return?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. = NULL always evaluates to NULL (not TRUE), so no rows are returned. This is SQL's famous NULL gotcha — always use IS NULL.

mcqs.question: Which matches a pattern containing a literal %?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The ESCAPE clause designates \\ as the escape character, so \% is a literal %. Option A is ambiguous without ESCAPE. Option C has %% which just means 'any two sequences of any length.'

mcqs.question: What does COUNT(email) return if the email column has 10 values, 3 of which are NULL?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. COUNT(column) counts non-NULL values. 10 - 3 NULLs = 7. COUNT(*) would return 10.

mcqs.question: Which is the MySQL-specific function for 2-argument NULL substitution?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. IFNULL(x, default) is MySQL's 2-arg function. COALESCE is standard SQL and supports any number of arguments. ISNULL exists in SQL Server but not as a 2-arg substitution in MySQL.

mcqs.question: Which query finds names with second letter 'r'?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. One underscore (any first char), then 'r', then anything. Matches 'Priya' (P-r...), not 'Rohan' (starts with R so position 2 is 'o').

mcqs.question: What does AVG(marks) return when 3 of 10 marks are NULL?

options.text: Sum of all non-NULL marks divided by 10
Sum of all non-NULL marks divided by 7
Sum including NULLs as 0, divided by 10

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. AVG skips NULLs — both in sum and in count. If you want NULLs treated as 0, use AVG(COALESCE(marks, 0)) to include them in the denominator.

mcqs.question: In PostgreSQL, which is equivalent to MySQL's case-insensitive LIKE 'a%'?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. PostgreSQL's LIKE is case-sensitive. ILIKE is the case-insensitive version (PostgreSQL-specific). MySQL uses collation to determine case sensitivity.

mcqs.question: What does CONCAT('Hello ', NULL, ' World') return in MySQL?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. In MySQL's CONCAT, any NULL argument makes the whole result NULL. Use CONCAT_WS (skips NULLs) or wrap in COALESCE.

mcqs.question: Why is WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT student_id FROM banned) dangerous?

options.text: If banned.student_id has any NULL, the query returns 0 rows
NOT IN is banned in modern SQL

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. NULL in the subquery result turns NOT IN into NULL comparisons, which WHERE drops. Use NOT EXISTS or filter NULLs in the subquery.

mcqs.question: What does NULL = NULL evaluate to in SQL?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Any comparison involving NULL yields NULL — even NULL = NULL. This is three-valued logic. Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL for NULL tests.

mcqs.question: Why might a pattern like LIKE '%abc' be slow on a million-row table?

options.text: Leading % wildcard cannot use a B-tree index; requires full scan

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. B-tree indexes work left-to-right. A leading wildcard means MySQL does not know where to start, so it scans every row. Trailing wildcards (LIKE 'abc%') CAN use indexes. For leading-wildcard search at scale, use full-text indexes.

mcqs.question: What is the safest way to compute salary / bonus when bonus might be 0?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. NULLIF(bonus, 0) returns NULL when bonus = 0. Dividing by NULL gives NULL (no error). In MySQL, divide-by-zero returns NULL already, but NULLIF makes intent explicit and is portable. Option B would still divide by 0 if bonus is 0.

codingChallenges.title: Gmail Finder
codingChallenges.description: List all students with a Gmail address, showing name and email.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput:
Aarav Sharma aarav@gmail.com
Rohan Gupta rohan@gmail.com
Sneha Iyer sneha@gmail.com
Aditya Rao aditya@gmail.com
codingChallenges.solution: SELECT name, email FROM students WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com';

codingChallenges.title: Missing Data Report
codingChallenges.description: List students with missing email OR missing marks. Show name and why they are missing data.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Ananya Singh, Vikram Reddy, Sneha Iyer (city missing), Ishita Kumar
codingChallenges.constraints: Use IS NULL with OR. (Simpler version: just missing email OR marks, 4 rows.)
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT name,
  CASE
    WHEN email IS NULL AND marks IS NULL THEN 'Missing email and marks'
    WHEN email IS NULL THEN 'Missing email'
    WHEN marks IS NULL THEN 'Missing marks'
  END AS issue
FROM students
WHERE email IS NULL OR marks IS NULL;

codingChallenges.title: Name Length Filter
codingChallenges.description: Find students whose name (first + last with space) is exactly 11 characters long.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Priya Patel, Rohan Gupta, Karan Mehta
codingChallenges.solution: SELECT name FROM students WHERE name LIKE '___________';  -- 11 underscores

codingChallenges.title: Clean Display
codingChallenges.description: Return name, email (show 'N/A' if NULL), marks (show 0 if NULL), and city (show 'Unknown' if NULL).
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: All 10 students with NULL values replaced.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
  name,
  COALESCE(email, 'N/A') AS email,
  COALESCE(marks, 0) AS marks,
  COALESCE(city, 'Unknown') AS city
FROM students;

codingChallenges.title: Complex Name Pattern
codingChallenges.description: Find students whose name starts with 'A' or 'R' AND has at least 12 characters.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Aarav Sharma (12 chars), Ananya Singh (12), Rohan Gupta (11 - no), Aditya Rao (10 - no)... Actually checking: 'Aarav Sharma' = 12, 'Ananya Singh' = 12. Those pass.

codingChallenges.title: Grade Report With Nulls
codingChallenges.description: Show each student's name and grade: A (>=85), B (>=70), C (<70), Absent (NULL marks).
codingChallenges.constraints: Use CASE with IS NULL first.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT name, marks,
  CASE
    WHEN marks IS NULL THEN 'Absent'
    WHEN marks >= 85 THEN 'A'
    WHEN marks >= 70 THEN 'B'
    ELSE 'C'
  END AS grade
FROM students;

codingChallenges.title: Safe Division
codingChallenges.description: Given a students_test table with attempted_questions and correct_questions, compute accuracy = correct / attempted, safely handling cases where attempted = 0. Write the SELECT.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: accuracy column is NULL when attempted = 0, otherwise ratio.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  correct_questions,
  attempted_questions,
  correct_questions / NULLIF(attempted_questions, 0) AS accuracy
FROM students_test;

codingChallenges.title: Pattern + NULL Combo
codingChallenges.description: Find students who (a) have a gmail email, (b) have non-NULL marks, (c) scored above 70, AND (d) are from a city starting with 'M' or 'D'.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Aarav Sharma (Mumbai, gmail, 85), Rohan Gupta (Delhi, gmail, 91)
codingChallenges.constraints: Combine LIKE, IS NOT NULL, comparison.
codingChallenges.solution:
SELECT name, email, marks, city
FROM students
WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com'
  AND marks IS NOT NULL
  AND marks > 70
  AND (city LIKE 'M%' OR city LIKE 'D%');
